I have laid out the most important things from Rimini Street's Q3 2025 results in the table above. I want to discuss why the company's performance supports a more bullish perspective, not the slight dip we've had since the end of October.

First, the revenue decline is barely noticeable, at 1% YoY, but it is vital that we remember the reason for it: RMNI is winding down its legacy PeopleSoft support. And we can see that the business itself is in pretty good shape, as indicated by its Adjusted Calculated Billings growing 6.7% compared to the same period last year. RPOs are also up to $611 million, a 9.3% increase when you take out PeopleSoft-related backlog.

So, we have a higher backlog, a good renewal base, and the company's active client count is up to 3,155. None of these indicators fit the narrative that a slight revenue and EPS miss seems to be driving.

Further on from that, let us look at the margins. Gross margin is still around 60% from both a GAAP and non-GAAP perspective, so it's clear that there's nothing wrong on an operational level. I must point out that operating income and adjusted EBITDA, both non-GAAP, dipped considerably compared to last year's numbers, but that's likely a consequence of the timing around service delivery, along with higher operating expense absorption.

In my opinion, these are unlikely to continue in Q4, and even though the adjusted profitability metrics are softer, they don't affect the foundation of my Buy position. It just means that now, I may have to adjust my EBITDA expectations for the full fiscal year.

Finally, we have the balance sheet, which is in much better shape than in Q2. Cash sits at ~$109 million, and the company has paid off its remaining revolver. That leaves only ~$70 million in long-term debt, so RMNI is basically in a net cash position now. Remember that this is a company that was trading like it might need to raise more capital just a few years ago, when it was waist-deep in litigation.

All of that is in the background now, and RMNI is using this newfound freedom to increase value for its shareholders. The company spent $3.8 million in Q3 on buying back around 900,000 shares, and I like that it has extended the repurchase authorization through 2029. There's well over $40 million left that can be spent on buybacks, and it's a very useful lever for management, given today's cheap multiples on its stock.

My Position Is Unchanged

In addition to everything that I outlined above, I'm very confident that RMNI will remain a strong performer because of its fundamentals. It's doing well with the transition into managed services, third-party cloud support, and AI-enabled ERP advisory, and there's nothing in Q3's numbers that contradicts that.

The only thing we really need to watch out for, at least in the short term, is profitability. I mentioned the dip in earnings earlier, and it means that the ramp I was expecting will be slower. As such, I'm also willing to adjust my reasoning, and the fix is straightforward. Let us assume that instead of full-year EBITDA snapping back toward the low 50s, it only comes out to ~$45 million for FY25. Then we wait for the cost base to reset, and the PeopleSoft runoff loses its impact.

That's the tighter setup I'm going for, and the rest of my thesis still holds. Now, let us look at the valuation today.

RMNI Is Still Cheap For What It Is

In my view, RMNI is still trading too cheaply for what it is, and today's valuation is still in investors' favor. Again, this is a business in a net cash position of ~$38 million, going off the debt and cash figures in the Q3 report. Its market cap is $353 million, and its enterprise value is $337 million, per Seeking Alpha's calculations.

So, the stock is trading at 6-7x EV/EBITDA if we use a more deliberate FY25 target of ~$45 million in EBITDA. The table I compiled in the earlier section already shows us why the multiple looks out of line because the fundamentals don't support the idea that the business deserves such a distressed multiple. They show, quite clearly, that RMNI's business model justifies trading closer to 9 or 10x EBITDA in a normal environment.

Here's how I see the upside, going with some realistic assumptions.

  • Base Case (8.5x EBITDA on $47 million)

Here, I'm assuming that the market closes the valuation gap as the company's profitability is more stable. We get an EV of ~$400 million, and the fair value of the stock comes out to ~$4.8/share.

  • Bull Case (9.5x EBITDA on $50 million)

I'm assuming that RMNI achieves better operating leverage as the runoff from legacy PeopleSoft fades and managed services continue to expand.

Fair value: ~$5.6/share

  • Bear Case (7x EBITDA on $45 million)

Basically, the market is pricing in one or two more quarters of margin pressure.

Fair value: ~$3.85/share

That's not far off today's price, so the spread here is very favorable. I say the downside is already covered by the company's net cash position, steady gross margins, and sticky recurring revenue. What we need to see here is a modest uptick in the multiples.

Risks

I've already touched on the decline in adjusted profitability metrics and the drag from legacy runoffs, so I'll discuss other risks here. First, execution risk is still a consideration. The upside I've described largely depends on RMNI becoming more than just a pure maintenance business, and we're seeing very promising signs of a successful transition. However, it depends on consistent delivery, staffing, and client expansion, so the company needs to keep delivering on all those fronts.

Besides those, we must also remember that macrosensitivity is a key factor in RMNI's industry. A lot of the company's new offerings are tied to modernization projects, and if clients delay their decisions into later quarters, it affects the topline and margins. Fortunately, the company has a pretty hefty backlog, so I'm not too worried about this either.

Your Takeaway

Nothing in Q3's results changes my arguments for RMNI, and the stock sold off because the quarter missed a few targets. That's all, and it doesn't change my long-term outlook. The multiples are still cheap, and the cash position and buybacks give the stock a solid floor. Consequently, I'm reiterating my Buy recommendation.

data.title: Elbit Systems: Backlog Supports Growth, But Valuation And Cash Flows Limit Upside
data.description:
Elbit Systems (ESLT) stock price has substantially increased in value since my buy rating in August 2024 surging 155% against the S&P 500’s 18.8% gain making it one of the aerospace and defense top performers. However, in September I downgraded the stock from buy to hold and we see that the stock gained just 0.5% against the 1.2% gain for the S&P 500. In this report, I discuss why Elbit System has been underperforming, analyze the Q3 2025 earnings and I update my stock price target and explain why I believe the stock remains a hold.

Growth Narrative Shifts From Israel To International Defense Sales

If we look beyond the performance since September, we can only conclude that the stock price performance has been impressive. The major outperformance against the market performance eventually can also lead to underperformance. However, for Elbit there is also a shift in the growth dynamics. Demand for weapon systems remains elevated globally. However, previously Elbit Systems benefited from increased weapon system sales to Israel in support of the war in Gaza and with a ceasefire in place and some relative stability in the region the growth prospects for sales to the IDF are fading and shifting more towards international sales.

Yaacov Kagan, CFO of Elbit Systems, said the following about that during the Q3 2025 earnings call:

We are targeting around flattish backlog in Israel and growth outside of Israel, predominantly in Europe. That will be the growth area, which -- we see our funnel, we see our opportunities, and we see the demand that's coming out from Europe. And we think that this is the place that predominantly will provide the growth in the future in the backlog.

That is not to say that sales to Israel are going to implode or decline necessarily, but there may be some normalization in the share of sales to Israel. For the first nine months of the year, the share of sales to Israel was 33.2% compared to 28.5% in the first nine months of 2024 and 29.1% for the full year. With the growth shifting, we are just going to see more sales going to come from European demand fulfilment.

Elbit Systems Sales And Margins Grow

During the third quarter, sales grew 12% to $1.9 billion. Aerospace sales declined modestly by 2.9% to $477.7 million. C4I and Cyber sales grew 14% to $243.3 million, ISTAR and EW which includes Intelligence, Surveillance, Target Acquisition and Reconnaissance and Electronic Warfare grew 5.2% to $593.7 million, Land Systems sales grew 40.8% to $593.7 million, and Elbit Systems of America sales declined 2.1% to $407.7 million. In Aerospace, sales declined due to lower precision guided munition sales while C4I and Cyber sales grew due to radio systems and C2 (Command & Control) sales in Europe. ISTAR and EW sales growth was driven by higher electro-optic and electronic warfare sales in Israel while Land revenues were driven by munition demand from Europe and Israel. Sales in the US decreased due to lower electronic system sales partially offset by higher demand for maritime and warfighter system sales.

Operating income grew 33% to $187 million indicating margins of 9.7% compared to 8.2% a year ago. Operating cash flow increased by $100 million to $157 million while free cash flow increased by $96 million to $101 million and backlog grew 14% to $25.2 billion. So, we do see margin improvement, better cash conversion and significant backlog.

Order Backlog Supports 3.3x Annual Sales

While the backlog composition is expected to start leaning increasingly on international sales and primarily sales to Europe, we note that the backlog growth indicating substantial upside to revenues. Backlog has grown 18% annually since 2020 while revenues grew “only” 11% and the current backlog of $25.2 billion covers 3.27x twelve-month trailing sales. Typically, I believe a healthy ballpark figure is a 2.5 to 3 year backlog coverage. For Elbit Systems, this would indicate $8.4 billion in annual sales at minimum and that would indicate support for around 9% sales growth. A 2.5 year coverage would indicate revenue potential north of $10 billion but that is something that I do not expect to happen until 2028.

Elbit Systems Stock Remains A Hold

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Elbit Systems, I am increasing my price target from $520.53 to $530.80. The increase is driven by higher expected EBITDA. Between 2025 and 2027, EBITDA estimates have increased 2.7% with a 0.8% downward revision in 2025%, up 2.5% in 2026 and up 5.5% in 2027. The CAGR has improved from 19% to 21.6%. Free cash flow estimates have decreased by 3.8% this year, up modestly for 2026 and down by 8.6% in 2027 as capital expenditures increase to facilitate sales growth in Europe. The total downward revision is 4.2% with a CAGR of 16.9%, down from 20.7% expected earlier. Given the single digit upside, the hold rating for Elbit Systems remains.

While I believe that the stock remains a hold and is not yet a buy, the underlying metrics are expected to trend well with continued margin growth as operating leverage improves further. One metric that does not impress at the moment is the cash flow. We see that operating cash flow growth is expected to flatten. So, the downward revision on free cash flow is not solely driven by higher capital expenditures. I believe the flattening in the operating cash flow growth in 2027 is primarily driven by the tapering of growth in cash flows from sales to the IDF. For 2026, the company is expected to enter a net cash position, which does open up room for dividend increases and inorganic growth opportunities.

Conclusion: Elbit Systems Stock Is Growth At A High Price

I believe that Elbit Systems is not necessarily unattractive, but I am not a big fan of the prospects of tapering operating cash flow growth and the current valuation. It results in relatively low upside for next year which also provides support to maintain my hold rating. Europe is likely going to be a growth area for Elbit Systems and the company will also invest to grow its presence in Europe, but it is not enough to warrant a buy rating.
